content.problemStatement:
Sorting the array [6,5,3,1] using bubble sort.

Initial List
[6,5,3,1]
First Pass:
Compare 6 and 5, swap -> [5,6,3,1]
Compare 6 and 3, swap -> [5,3,6,1]
Compare 6 and 1, swap -> [5,3,1,**6**]
Second Pass
Compare 5 and 3, swap -> [3,5,1,**6**]
Compare 5 and 1, swap -> [3,1,**5**,**6**]
Third Pass
Compare 3 and 1, swap -> [**1**,**3**,**5**,**6**]
The list is now sorted.
[1,3,6,5]
Task
Given a program to sort using bubble sort.
Analyze the code for bubble sort and **submit**.
content.inputFormat: The first line contains an integer T, the number of test cases. For each test case, the first line contains an integer N, the number of elements. The second line contains N space‑separated integers.
content.outputFormat: For each test case output the N integers sorted in non‑decreasing order, separated by a single space. Print a newline after each test case.

sampleTestCases.explanation: The list [6,5,3,1] is sorted into ascending order using bubble sort.
The input list is already sorted; bubble sort leaves it unchanged.
Bubble sort swaps adjacent elements until the list becomes [1,2,3].

solution.approach: For each test case we apply the classic bubble sort algorithm. We repeatedly scan the array from the beginning, swapping adjacent elements whenever they are out of order. After the i‑th pass the largest i elements are guaranteed to be at the end, so the inner loop can stop at N‑i‑1. The process continues for N‑1 passes, which is sufficient to fully sort the array. The algorithm uses only constant extra space.
solution.timeComplexity: O(N^2) per test case, because there are two nested loops each up to N.
solution.spaceComplexity: O(1) auxiliary space (the array itself is modified in place).
